title: How Black Holes Erase Quantum Secrets
oneliner: Photons falling into a black hole can destroy a quantum particle's ability to be in two places at once.
description: A quantum particle can be like a spinning coin—both heads and tails until observed. Near a black hole, a passing photon acts like a camera flash, forcing the particle to 'land' on one state. That photon then crosses the event horizon, adding a snapshot to the black hole's surface memory without breaking information storage rules.
text: A quantum particle can be in two states at once—like a coin spinning, both heads and tails. Near a [tag:black_hole]black hole[/tag], the event horizon—where [tag:spacetime_curvature]spacetime curvature[/tag] bends space and time to the extreme—acts as a one-way gate. A stray photon grazing this boundary 'photographs' the coin, forcing it to land on one side. The photon then crosses the horizon, carrying the snapshot into oblivion. Black holes store information on their surface, not inside—like a hologram. The photon's record gets added to the horizon's area, tweaking only the electromagnetic field without adding mass or energy. This obeys strict [tag:entropy]entropy[/tag] laws discovered by [scientist:Jacob Bekenstein]Jacob Bekenstein[/scientist] and [scientist:Stephen Hawking]Stephen Hawking[/scientist]. The twist: a black hole's memory lives on a two-dimensional surface, a cosmic canvas where quantum possibilities vanish without a trace.
fun_fact: A black hole's information capacity is measured by its surface area, not its volume—a counterintuitive result that emerged from Bekenstein and Hawking's work.
mini: A spinning coin is both heads and tails—until photographed. Near a black hole, passing photons act like a camera, forcing a quantum particle to pick one state. The 'photo' falls into the hole, vanishing forever. This explains how black holes erase quantum fuzziness while respecting cosmic memory rules.
